# Biga

Tooling to build out design tokens for use in UIs across dbt Labs.

## What is biga?

> biga: a thick mixture of flour, water, and yeast that is allowed to ferment for some time before being combined with fresh dough to make bread, used especially in Italian baking

tl;dr The goal of this repo is to take a direct export of our design system variables and transform them into something usable in code.

For a deep dive into how we got to this solution, take a look at design's [Tokens](https://www.notion.so/dbtlabs/Tokens-d421533271704c8f816a6af4bfff6678#773d81b01cd44f9dac3e01e8c3cd6d49) and [Design Token Impl](https://www.notion.so/dbtlabs/Design-Tokens-Impl-5b8899e7a25844bc83f417cf36c9e178).

It contains a suite of "processor" utility functions that make that happen.
The exports from our design system are bloated, inconsistent and out of our control (for now).
Our tooling makes it so that we can _consistently_ take the unmodified exported assets from figma as inputs,
clean them up and output a set of js/css assets to easily import into consuming apps.

The general flow goes something like this:

1. export the figma variables via a plugin (we use [this one](https://github.com/lukasoppermann/design-tokens) currently)
2. copy those into `biga/design-assets`
3. run the build script with `pnpm build`
4. commit everything including the newly generated `tokens/*` assets
5. release a new version of this package like any other bakery package (including changeset, PR, chore PR etc)
6. bump the biga version in the consuming packages/apps and the new token values will be there

We have plans to eventually automate steps 2-5 via webhooks... one day.

## Biga internals

This package is largely a set of processor or transformation functions that plug into the ecosystem of [StyleDictionary](https://styledictionary.com/getting-started/installation/).
The main concepts are:

- `preprocessor`: pre-process the input files before processing into tokens
- `transformers`: hook into the compilation steps to define the tokens
- `filters`: filter out unwanted tokens
- `formatters`: take the transformed tokens and format them into some output asset

The docs have a great overview of each under the [API > Hooks](https://styledictionary.com/getting-started/installation/) section if you want to learn more.

Our processors live under `src/processors` and each are registered onto the `StyleDictionary` class
inside of `build.ts`.
Then a `config` ([docs](https://styledictionary.com/reference/config/)) object defines which processors are used for which "platforms". Each platform has its own configuration and outputs a single asset.
e.g. we have a `css/raw` platform that receives the transformed tokens and exports a `tokens.css` asset.

## Design asset inputs

Note that we have two separate design-asset exports we use as inputs.
Each represents a different part of our design token system and reference each other to allow us to build a robust theming system.

**primitives.json**

Exported from the [dbt Color system](https://www.figma.com/design/U6ZaNF0kmXvVdUvHAc0chK/dbt-Color-system).
This contains the primitive color _values_ and their associated color _alias_ names.
Example: The `neutrals.900` alias has a hex value of `#111827`

**tokens.json**

Exported from the [dbt Design system](<https://www.figma.com/design/osn6heqyx0nPtG7s6ti6bE/Pumpernickel-(dbt-cloud-ui)>) aka Pumpernickel.
This contains the semantic token names, by mode/theme and what primitive alias they use for that mode.
Example: The token `bgPrimary` uses `neutrals.white` for light mode and `neutrals.900` for dark mode.

## Final result

We export several files for use in consuming apps and can be found in `biga/tokens/`.

> We are only covering color tokens at the moment, but we plan to include number/string tokens in the near future! Also these docs only refer to light/dark mode but we should be able to extend to
> additional modes with slight modifications.

**tokens.css**

This contains the tokens as css variables nested under each `root:` mode.

```css
:root {
  --bgPrimary: '#ffffffff';
}

:root.dark {
  --bgPrimary: #111827ff;
}
```

> We use the existence of an ancestor element containing a `.dark` class to denote the variable values should switch to the dark values. For future modes it would be the same process just using a different class e.g. `:root.high-contrast`. Everything else should just work.

**tokens.(c)js**

A JS mapping of the tokens and the associated css variable.
We use this to spread into our tailwind.config's to automatically plug into the tailwind color ecosystem.

```js
{
  bgPrimary: 'var(--bgPrimary)`
}
```

This allows us to leverage TW class names with our colors e.g. `bg-bgPrimary` or `text-fgPrimary` or `border-borderPrimary` etc.

**token-mappings.md**

Provides additional notes meant to aide the migration from existing TW color classes to our tokens.
We map existing color values from existing TW usage to the newly transformed token values to create a
table that can be used as a reference for which tokens should be used where.

Example:
If Joe is migrating a component that uses `text-gray-900 dark:text-white`, what token should they use going forward?
If they look at the token-mappings.md table they will see the `gray-900` has a match with `fgPrimary`.
They should feel _fairly confident_ that they can now use `text-fgPrimary` and drop the `dark:` class.

> Note: Our existing code may have been wrong all along so we cannot be 100% sure finding a match in these tables will work. You may not even find a matching token for an existing class in this table. It is just meant to be an aide while we migrate.

There is separate table in this file covering the `active` and `accent` classes.
These exist in our code base today but we don't use them in our underlying color system anymore
so they may require special handling.
Reach out to a designer if you get stuck picking a proper token to replace them!

## Using biga in your app

At minimum, import the `tokens.css` file into a css file that will land in the compiled app.
Then use those variables where needed and when you want the token values to switch to that mode,
add the appropriate class to some ancestor element e.g. `<body class="dark">...</body>`.

```css
/* in your-app.css */
@import '@dbt-labs/biga/tokens.css';
```

If you want to use with tailwind, also import/require the `tokens.js` in your config and
spread the tokens into the `config.theme.extend.colors` object.

```js
// your-app/tailwind.config.cjs
const { tokens } = require('@dbt-labs/biga');

module.exports = {
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: {
        ...tokens,
      },
    },
  },
};
```

Also, if your ui module has storybook, add the following line to your `.storybook/preview.tsx`:

```
import '@dbt-labs/biga/tokens.css';
```
